title: AcyclicNonCollidersBlockable

tex_statement.html: If $G$ is acyclic then all non-colliders are blockable.

tex_proof.raw:
% Claim proof file for row claim_3_20 -- "AcyclicNonCollidersBlockable".
% Auto-generated stub by scaffold/solving_current_row.py at row start. A
% manager agent dedicated to writing the TeX proof fills in the body below.
% The proof must:
%   - Stay close to the lecture notes' proof if one exists (always search
%     the LN first for a `\begin{proof}` block immediately following the
%     claim; copy / lightly edit when present).
%   - Otherwise use the LN paradigm and cite prior claims by their `ref`.
%   - Be self-contained enough that a mathematician can follow it without
%     re-reading the LN.
%   - Have no `sorry`, `omitted`, or "obvious" shortcuts.
%
% Compiles standalone via the `subfiles` package.

\documentclass[main]{subfiles}

\begin{document}
% ref: claim_3_20 (proof, with statement restated for readability)
% title: AcyclicNonCollidersBlockable
\def\rowref{claim\_3\_20}
\phantomsection\label{claim_3_20}
%
% Cross-references: see claim_<ref>_statement_<title>.tex in this folder
% for the corresponding statement.

% --- statement (restated) ---------------------------------------------------
\begin{Rem}[AcyclicNonCollidersBlockable]
If $G$ is acyclic then all non-colliders are blockable.
\end{Rem}

% --- proof ------------------------------------------------------------------
\begin{proof}
Let $v_k$ be a non-collider on $\pi$. By def 3.16, $v_k$ is a blockable non-collider on $\pi$ if and only if it is a non-collider on $\pi$ and is \emph{not} an unblockable non-collider on $\pi$. The non-collider clause already holds by hypothesis, so it suffices to show that $v_k$ is not unblockable on $\pi$.

Suppose for contradiction that $v_k$ were an unblockable non-collider on $\pi$. Then, by def 3.16, $k \notin \lC 0, n\rC$ (so both neighbours $v_{k-1}$ and $v_{k+1}$ exist on $\pi$) and the joint at $v_k$ falls into one of the three patterns
\[\begin{array}{rccl}
  \text{ left chain: } & v_{k-1} \hut v_k \hus v_{k+1} & \text{ with } & v_{k-1} \in \Sc^G(v_k),\\
  \text{ right chain: } & v_{k-1} \suh v_k \tuh v_{k+1} & \text{ with } & v_{k+1} \in \Sc^G(v_k),\\
  \text{ fork: } & v_{k-1} \hut v_k \tuh v_{k+1} & \text{ with } & v_{k-1} \in \Sc^G(v_k) \land v_{k+1} \in \Sc^G(v_k).
\end{array}\]
By def 3.5(7), $\Sc^G(v_k) = \Anc^G(v_k) \cap \Desc^G(v_k) \ins \Anc^G(v_k)$, so any neighbour of $v_k$ on $\pi$ landing in $\Sc^G(v_k)$ in particular admits a directed walk into $v_k$.

In the \emph{left chain} pattern the edge $v_{k-1} \hut v_k$ is the directed edge $v_k \tuh v_{k-1}$ in $G$, i.e.\ a directed walk from $v_k$ to $v_{k-1}$ of length $1$. Combined with the directed walk $v_{k-1} \to \cdots \to v_k$ witnessing $v_{k-1} \in \Anc^G(v_k)$, this concatenates to a directed walk from $v_k$ to itself of length at least $1$, contradicting acyclicity of $G$ (def 3.6).

The \emph{right chain} pattern is symmetric: $v_k \tuh v_{k+1}$ is a directed edge $v_k \to v_{k+1}$, and $v_{k+1} \in \Sc^G(v_k) \ins \Anc^G(v_k)$ supplies a directed walk $v_{k+1} \to \cdots \to v_k$, so concatenation again yields a directed walk from $v_k$ to itself of length at least $1$, contradicting def 3.6.

In the \emph{fork} pattern both arms point outward from $v_k$ and both endpoints lie in $\Sc^G(v_k)$, so either side closes a directed cycle through $v_k$ exactly as in the left or right chain case, contradicting def 3.6.

In all three cases we reach a contradiction. Therefore $v_k$ is not an unblockable non-collider on $\pi$, and combined with the non-collider hypothesis we conclude that $v_k$ is a blockable non-collider on $\pi$.
\end{proof}

\end{document}
tex_proof.html:
Remark — AcyclicNonCollidersBlockable

If $G$ is acyclic then all non-colliders are blockable.

Proof.

Let $v_k$ be a non-collider on $\pi$. By def 3.16, $v_k$ is a blockable non-collider on $\pi$ if and only if it is a non-collider on $\pi$ and is not an unblockable non-collider on $\pi$. The non-collider clause already holds by hypothesis, so it suffices to show that $v_k$ is not unblockable on $\pi$.

Suppose for contradiction that $v_k$ were an unblockable non-collider on $\pi$. Then, by def 3.16, $k \notin \lC 0, n\rC$ (so both neighbours $v_{k-1}$ and $v_{k+1}$ exist on $\pi$) and the joint at $v_k$ falls into one of the three patterns \[\begin{array}{rccl} \text{ left chain: } & v_{k-1} \hut v_k \hus v_{k+1} & \text{ with } & v_{k-1} \in \Sc^G(v_k),\\ \text{ right chain: } & v_{k-1} \suh v_k \tuh v_{k+1} & \text{ with } & v_{k+1} \in \Sc^G(v_k),\\ \text{ fork: } & v_{k-1} \hut v_k \tuh v_{k+1} & \text{ with } & v_{k-1} \in \Sc^G(v_k) \land v_{k+1} \in \Sc^G(v_k). \end{array}\] By def 3.5(7), $\Sc^G(v_k) = \Anc^G(v_k) \cap \Desc^G(v_k) \ins \Anc^G(v_k)$, so any neighbour of $v_k$ on $\pi$ landing in $\Sc^G(v_k)$ in particular admits a directed walk into $v_k$.

In the left chain pattern the edge $v_{k-1} \hut v_k$ is the directed edge $v_k \tuh v_{k-1}$ in $G$, i.e.\ a directed walk from $v_k$ to $v_{k-1}$ of length $1$. Combined with the directed walk $v_{k-1} \to \cdots \to v_k$ witnessing $v_{k-1} \in \Anc^G(v_k)$, this concatenates to a directed walk from $v_k$ to itself of length at least $1$, contradicting acyclicity of $G$ (def 3.6).

The right chain pattern is symmetric: $v_k \tuh v_{k+1}$ is a directed edge $v_k \to v_{k+1}$, and $v_{k+1} \in \Sc^G(v_k) \ins \Anc^G(v_k)$ supplies a directed walk $v_{k+1} \to \cdots \to v_k$, so concatenation again yields a directed walk from $v_k$ to itself of length at least $1$, contradicting def 3.6.

In the fork pattern both arms point outward from $v_k$ and both endpoints lie in $\Sc^G(v_k)$, so either side closes a directed cycle through $v_k$ exactly as in the left or right chain case, contradicting def 3.6.

In all three cases we reach a contradiction. Therefore $v_k$ is not an unblockable non-collider on $\pi$, and combined with the non-collider hypothesis we conclude that $v_k$ is a blockable non-collider on $\pi$.

lean_code_with_comments:
/-- claim_3_20 (`AcyclicNonCollidersBlockable`): if `G` is acyclic
then every non-collider position on every walk in `G` is a
*blockable* non-collider. Mirrors
`lecture-notes/lecture_notes/graphs.tex` claim_3_20 (the
`\begin{claimmark}\begin{Rem}...\end{Rem}\end{claimmark}` block at
lines 1256 -- 1261) verbatim, with the LN's "all non-colliders"
unrolled under the def_3_16 paradigm to a universal quantifier over
walks `π` and positions `k`, and the non-collider antecedent kept as
the hypothesis `hk : π.IsNonColliderAt k`. -/
theorem isBlockableNonColliderAt_of_isNonColliderAt_of_isAcyclic
    (hG : G.IsAcyclic) {v w : α} (π : Walk G v w) {k : ℕ}
    (hk : π.IsNonColliderAt k) :
    π.IsBlockableNonColliderAt k :=
  ⟨hk, not_isUnblockableNonColliderAt_of_isAcyclic hG π k⟩
lean_code_without_comments:
theorem isBlockableNonColliderAt_of_isNonColliderAt_of_isAcyclic
    (hG : G.IsAcyclic) {v w : α} (π : Walk G v w) {k : ℕ}
    (hk : π.IsNonColliderAt k) :
    π.IsBlockableNonColliderAt k :=
  ⟨hk, not_isUnblockableNonColliderAt_of_isAcyclic hG π k⟩
lean_explanation:
The theorem `isBlockableNonColliderAt_of_isNonColliderAt_of_isAcyclic` formalises claim 3.20 of the lecture notes: in an acyclic CDMG, every non-collider position on every walk is a *blockable* non-collider. Read aloud, the Lean signature says: assume the graph `G : CDMG α` is acyclic; fix a walk $\pi$ from $v$ to $w$ in `G`; fix a position $k$ along $\pi$ that is a non-collider; then $k$ is a blockable non-collider on $\pi$.

The binders map to the LN as follows. `α : Type*` is the ambient vertex type, matching the LN's silent assumption of a fixed carrier. `G : CDMG α` is the conditional directed mixed graph — the LN's $G$. The walk endpoints `v w : α` are left implicit (Lean infers them from $\pi$); the walk `π : Walk G v w` is explicit (callers pass it positionally); and the position `k : ℕ` is implicit, since the non-collider hypothesis `hk : π.IsNonColliderAt k` already names it. The position predicates `IsNonColliderAt`, `IsBlockableNonColliderAt`, and (its negation-companion) `IsUnblockableNonColliderAt` are project-local: they live on `Walk` and take a `ℕ` position, mirroring the LN's def 3.15 / def 3.16 paradigm of predicates on a single walk plus a single position on it.

The body is a one-line anonymous constructor `⟨hk, not_isUnblockableNonColliderAt_of_isAcyclic hG π k⟩`. The `IsBlockableNonColliderAt` predicate is by definition the conjunction *non-collider* $\land$ *not unblockable*, so the proof simply supplies its two components. The first is the hypothesis itself; the second is delegated to a private helper that consumes acyclicity and rules out any `IsUnblockableNonColliderAt` witness. The helper proceeds by recursion on the walk constructor, isolating the LN proof's three patterns (left chain, right chain, fork) as the single `cons s (cons s' _), 1` case and discharging the remaining positions either by `nomatch`-style elimination or by recursion on the walk tail. In each pattern, the `Sc ⊆ Anc` inclusion supplied by the joint condition produces a directed walk back to the joint vertex, and prepending the strict outgoing edge closes a directed cycle, contradicting `G.IsAcyclic`.
design_choices:
**Positive conclusion, not a negation.** The LN states the conclusion as *blockable* — the positive predicate `IsBlockableNonColliderAt` — and we mirror that vocabulary exactly. An equivalent encoding would drop the non-collider hypothesis and conclude `¬ π.IsUnblockableNonColliderAt k` directly: the two shapes are inter-derivable, since at non-non-collider positions the unblockability predicate is already vacuously false, so dropping the antecedent does not actually weaken the statement. We reject the negated shape for two reasons. First, LN-lexical fidelity: keeping the conclusion lined up with the LN's wording word-for-word matters when the theorem is itself a translation. Second, consumer ergonomics: the downstream definitions of $\sigma$-blocked walks and $i\sigma$-separation quantify universally over *blockable* non-collider positions in a conditioning set $C$, so they want a conclusion that already says `IsBlockableNonColliderAt` and can be discharged under that quantifier by modus ponens, with no extra unpacking at the call site.

**Pointwise universal closure, walk explicit and position implicit.** The LN's phrasing *all non-colliders are blockable* reads naturally under the def 3.16 paradigm (a predicate on a single walk and a single position) as: for every walk $\pi$ in $G$ and every position $k$ on $\pi$, `IsNonColliderAt k → IsBlockableNonColliderAt k`. We quantify $\pi$ explicitly so callers pass it positionally, and $k$ implicitly so the elaborator can infer it from the hypothesis. The non-collider antecedent is kept as a hypothesis rather than absorbed into the conclusion, because the implication is genuinely conditional — a generic position $k$ need not be a non-collider in the first place.

**One-way implication only.** The LN claims acyclic $\Rightarrow$ all non-colliders blockable, and that is the direction we formalise. The converse also holds — any directed cycle yields, on a length-2 walk that traverses the cycle, an unblockable non-collider at the apex — but no downstream row in the formalisation consumes the converse, so we leave the biconditional for a future row that genuinely requires it. Bundling both directions speculatively would mean carrying a proof that no caller invokes.

**Per-walk, not per-vertex.** The theorem fixes a single walk and a single position on it; it does not directly assert *for every walk through a vertex $v$, the visit at $v$ is blockable*. The two formulations are inter-derivable, and every downstream consumer already quantifies over walks, so the per-walk shape composes cleanly. This choice is forced by paradigm consistency with def 3.15 and def 3.16, both of which already define collider-ness and blockability *on* a walk plus a position rather than on a vertex.
